7.description: TCP/UDP Echo service — rarely needed, can be used in amplification attacks.
recommendations: Disable the echo service
Block at firewall

21.description: File Transfer Protocol — transmits credentials and data in cleartext.
recommendations: Replace with SFTP (SSH port 22) or FTPS
Disable anonymous access
Restrict to known IP ranges
Implement account lockout

22.description: Secure Shell — encrypted remote access. Safe when configured correctly; commonly brute-forced.
risks: Default port makes it a high-scan-volume target
recommendations: Disable password authentication; require SSH keys
Change default port from 22 to reduce automated scans
Implement fail2ban or similar rate-limiting
Restrict SSH access via firewall to known IP ranges
Keep OpenSSH updated to the latest stable version
Disable root login (PermitRootLogin no)

23.description: Legacy remote access protocol — sends everything including passwords in PLAINTEXT. Should never be exposed.
risks: All traffic including credentials transmitted in cleartext
Easily sniffed on any shared network segment
No integrity protection — vulnerable to MITM
recommendations: Disable Telnet immediately and replace with SSH
If Telnet is required for legacy devices, isolate on a dedicated VLAN with strict ACLs
Block port 23 at the perimeter firewall

25.description: Email transfer protocol. Open relays allow spam and phishing campaigns.
recommendations: Require STARTTLS or use SMTPS (port 465)
Disable open relay — only relay for authenticated/internal users
Disable VRFY and EXPN commands
Rate-limit authentication attempts

53.description: Domain Name System. Open resolvers can be abused for amplification DDoS attacks.
risks: Open resolver enables DNS amplification attacks (up to 70× amplification)
Zone transfer leaks internal network topology
recommendations: Restrict recursive queries to internal IPs only
Disable zone transfers to unauthorized IPs
Implement DNSSEC
Use response rate limiting (RRL)

80.description: Unencrypted web server. All traffic is visible to network observers.
risks: Information disclosure via error pages or headers
recommendations: Redirect all HTTP to HTTPS
Implement strict Content-Security-Policy
Keep web server and application frameworks updated
Disable directory listing
Remove server version headers

110.description: Post Office Protocol v3 — email retrieval. Cleartext without TLS.
recommendations: Migrate to POP3S (port 995) or IMAPS (993)
Disable plaintext POP3
Enforce TLS 1.2+

111.description: Remote Procedure Call portmapper — leaks internal service information and enables NFS attacks.
risks: Service enumeration — reveals all RPC services running
Gateway to NFS, NIS, and other dangerous services
recommendations: Block port 111 at the firewall — never expose to internet
If RPC is needed internally, restrict with tcpwrappers or iptables

135.description: Microsoft RPC endpoint mapper — used by many Windows services. Critical attack surface.
risks: Remote code execution via crafted RPC calls
recommendations: Block at firewall — never expose to internet
Apply all Windows security updates
Disable unnecessary DCOM services

139.description: NetBIOS Session Service — legacy Windows file sharing. Extremely dangerous when internet-exposed.
recommendations: Block ports 139 and 445 at the firewall
Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP if not needed
Apply MS17-010 patch immediately if unpatched
Disable SMBv1

143.description: Internet Message Access Protocol — email access. Cleartext without TLS.
recommendations: Enforce STARTTLS or migrate to IMAPS (993)
Disable plaintext IMAP
Rate-limit authentication attempts

179.description: Border Gateway Protocol — internet routing. Misconfiguration can cause major outages.
recommendations: Implement strict BGP filtering
Use RPKI for route origin validation
Enable MD5 authentication on BGP sessions
Never expose to internet without authentication

389.description: Lightweight Directory Access Protocol — directory services. Often contains sensitive user and credential data.
risks: Anonymous bind may expose entire directory
recommendations: Disable anonymous bind
Enforce LDAPS (port 636) or STARTTLS
Restrict access to internal networks only
Implement fine-grained access controls

443.description: Encrypted web server. Verify TLS configuration is current.
risks: Outdated TLS versions (SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1) still supported
recommendations: Enforce TLS 1.2+ only
Use strong cipher suites (ECDHE + AES-GCM)
Implement HSTS with preloading
Keep web server and TLS library updated
Enable OCSP stapling

445.description: Server Message Block — Windows file sharing. The most commonly exploited Windows protocol.
recommendations: NEVER expose SMB to the internet
Apply MS17-010 patch
Disable SMBv1 completely
Require SMB signing
Use Windows Defender Firewall to block external access

465.description: SMTP over TLS. Preferred over plaintext SMTP port 25 for submission.
recommendations: Enforce strong TLS cipher suites
Implement rate limiting on authentication attempts

514.description: Syslog UDP — log aggregation. Log injection and spoofing are trivial without authentication.
risks: Log spoofing — fake log entries can mask attacks
No authentication — anyone can inject logs
UDP is connectionless, trivial to spoof source IP
recommendations: Use encrypted/authenticated syslog (TLS, RFC 5425)
Restrict syslog receiver to internal IPs only
Never expose to internet

587.description: SMTP message submission port — requires authentication.
recommendations: Require STARTTLS
Implement multi-factor authentication on email accounts
Rate-limit failed authentication attempts

631.description: Internet Printing Protocol. Printers often have weak authentication and firmware vulnerabilities.
risks: Remote code execution via printer firmware CVEs
recommendations: Restrict IPP to internal network only
Enable authentication for print jobs
Keep printer firmware updated

873.description: Rsync file synchronization daemon. Anonymous rsync allows reading/writing entire filesystem shares.
risks: Anonymous read access to entire filesystem if misconfigured
Remote file write enables backdoor installation
recommendations: Require authentication for all rsync modules
Use rsync over SSH instead of native daemon
Restrict access to known IPs

990.description: FTP over TLS — more secure than plain FTP.
recommendations: Prefer SFTP (SSH) over FTPS for simplicity
Enforce TLS 1.2+

993.description: IMAP over TLS — encrypted email access.
recommendations: Implement rate limiting on authentication
Consider multi-factor authentication

995.description: POP3 over TLS — encrypted email retrieval.
recommendations: Implement rate limiting
Prefer IMAPS

1433.description: Microsoft SQL Server. Database servers should NEVER be internet-exposed.
risks: Direct database access — full data exfiltration possible
Brute-force of SA and other accounts
SQL injection escalation to OS-level access
recommendations: NEVER expose database ports to the internet
Use Windows authentication instead of SQL authentication
Disable xp_cmdshell
Place SQL Server behind application firewall
Enable SQL Server Audit

1723.description: Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol VPN — MS-CHAPv2 authentication is cryptographically broken.
risks: MS-CHAPv2 can be cracked with commodity hardware in under 24 hours
No traffic confidentiality after MS-CHAPv2 compromise
recommendations: Replace PPTP with IKEv2/IPSec, WireGuard, or OpenVPN immediately
PPTP provides false sense of security

1900.description: Universal Plug and Play SSDP. Should never be internet-exposed; enables device hijacking and SSRF.
recommendations: Disable UPnP on all internet-facing devices
Block port 1900 at the firewall

2049.description: Network File System. Anonymous NFS exports expose entire filesystems without authentication.
risks: Unauthenticated filesystem read/write if exports misconfigured
Root squashing bypass in some configurations
Complete data exfiltration of mounted shares
recommendations: NEVER expose NFS to the internet
Restrict /etc/exports to specific internal IPs
Enable root squashing
Use Kerberos authentication for NFS v4

2375.description: Docker daemon socket over TCP without TLS. Full container and host OS control with NO authentication.
risks: Trivial container escape to host root — mount host filesystem in container
Full host OS compromise via container escape
recommendations: NEVER expose Docker API to the internet
If remote access needed, use TLS mutual authentication (port 2376)
Use Docker socket proxy to restrict API access
Prefer Docker Swarm TLS or Kubernetes RBAC for cluster management

2376.description: Docker daemon over TLS. Secure if certificates are properly managed.
risks: Certificate management mistakes can expose API
Still provides full Docker control to authenticated clients
recommendations: Verify client certificate authentication is enforced
Rotate certificates regularly
Restrict to VPN or internal network

3000.description: Common development web server port (Node.js, Grafana, etc.). Development servers often lack production hardening.
recommendations: Restrict development servers to localhost or VPN
Do not expose development ports to the internet
If Grafana, change default admin/admin credentials immediately

3306.description: MySQL database. Database servers must NEVER be internet-exposed.
risks: Direct database access — full data exfiltration
Remote code execution via SELECT INTO OUTFILE and UDF loading
recommendations: NEVER expose MySQL to the internet
Bind to 127.0.0.1 or internal IP only (bind-address in my.cnf)
Use strong, unique passwords for all MySQL accounts
Disable remote root login
Enable MySQL audit log

3389.description: Windows Remote Desktop Protocol. One of the most exploited services on the internet.
risks: BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) — unauthenticated RCE on unpatched systems
MitM via Network Level Authentication bypass
recommendations: NEVER expose RDP to the internet — use VPN + RDP internally
Apply all Windows patches (especially KB4499175 for BlueKeep)
Enable Network Level Authentication (NLA)
Use strong passwords and account lockout
Implement MFA for RDP via Microsoft RD Gateway

4444.description: Default Metasploit Framework listener port. Indicates potential backdoor or active exploitation.
risks: Active penetration testing tool or backdoor shell
recommendations: Investigate immediately — this port should never be open in production
Check for unauthorized software and reverse shells
Audit running processes and established connections
Check for scheduled tasks and startup items

5432.description: PostgreSQL database. Must not be internet-exposed.
risks: COPY TO/FROM PROGRAM — OS command execution as postgres user
recommendations: NEVER expose PostgreSQL to the internet
Configure pg_hba.conf to restrict connections to localhost
Disable COPY PROGRAM if not needed
Use strong passwords for all database roles

5900.description: Virtual Network Computing — remote desktop. Often has weak or no authentication.
risks: Weak 8-character VNC password limit (easy to brute-force)
No encryption in VNC protocol itself
Screen capture and keyboard injection without logging
recommendations: NEVER expose VNC to the internet — tunnel over SSH instead
Use a strong VNC password
Enable VNC authentication (not 'None')
Prefer modern alternatives with MFA support

5984.description: CouchDB database with REST API. Frequently left with admin party mode enabled.
risks: Admin party mode — no authentication required, full admin access
Database enumeration and data exfiltration via REST API
Remote code execution via Erlang distribution port (CVE-2017-12636)
recommendations: Disable admin party mode immediately
Set strong admin credentials
Restrict access to internal network only
Disable Erlang distribution if not clustering

6379.description: Redis in-memory database. Default installation has NO authentication and binds to all interfaces.
risks: No authentication by default — full read/write access to all data
Remote code execution via CONFIG SET dir/dbfilename + SAVE (write SSH keys or cron jobs)
Data exfiltration of session tokens, cached credentials
recommendations: Set a strong requirepass in redis.conf
Bind to 127.0.0.1 only (bind 127.0.0.1)
NEVER expose Redis to the internet
Enable Redis ACLs (Redis 6+) for fine-grained access control
Disable CONFIG and DEBUG commands if not needed (rename-command)

8080.description: Alternate HTTP port — development, proxy, or application server.
risks: Often runs unpatched or development application servers
May expose admin panels without authentication
recommendations: Apply same hardening as port 80
Require authentication for any admin interfaces
Keep application server updated

8443.description: Alternate HTTPS port — often used for management interfaces or application servers.
risks: Management interfaces often have weak default credentials
recommendations: Restrict management interfaces to internal network or VPN
Change default credentials immediately
Enforce TLS 1.2+

9090.description: Common port for web-based management interfaces (Cockpit, Openfire, etc.).
risks: Management interfaces with weak default credentials
Admin panel exposure enables full system control
recommendations: Restrict to internal network or VPN
Change default credentials
Enable MFA

9200.description: Elasticsearch REST API. Default installation has NO authentication and exposes all indexed data.
risks: No authentication by default — full read/write to all indices
Data exfiltration of entire search index (often contains PII, logs, credentials)
Remote code execution via dynamic scripting in older versions
Ransomware targeting exposed Elasticsearch instances is common
recommendations: Enable X-Pack Security (built-in since Elasticsearch 8.0, free)
Set strong credentials for elastic superuser
Bind to internal IP only
NEVER expose to the internet
Review and audit index contents — may contain sensitive data

10000.description: Webmin system administration panel. CVE-2019-15107 was a backdoor injected into official source.
risks: CVE-2019-15107 — backdoor in Webmin 1.882-1.921 allows unauthenticated RCE
recommendations: Update to Webmin 1.930 or later
NEVER expose Webmin to the internet — restrict to VPN or localhost
Verify installation is from official, unmodified source
Consider replacing with more maintained alternatives

11211.description: Memcached cache server. No authentication, massive DDoS amplification factor (51,000×).
risks: No authentication — full cache read/write access
Data exfiltration of cached content (session tokens, user data)
Used in record-breaking DDoS amplification attacks (1.7 Tbps via UDP)
recommendations: NEVER expose Memcached to the internet
Bind to 127.0.0.1 only
Disable UDP if not needed (--disable-udp)
Enable SASL authentication

27017.description: MongoDB document database. Default installation binds to all interfaces with no authentication.
risks: No authentication by default in older versions — complete database access
Full data exfiltration of all collections
Ransom attacks common (databases wiped, ransom note left)
Remote code execution via server-side JavaScript in older versions
recommendations: Enable MongoDB authentication (--auth flag)
Create admin user before binding to network interfaces
Bind to 127.0.0.1 or internal IP only
Upgrade to MongoDB 6+ with authentication enabled by default
NEVER expose to the internet
Review MongoDB Atlas security configuration if using cloud

27018.description: MongoDB shard server port. Same risks as 27017.
recommendations: See MongoDB port 27017

28017.description: MongoDB built-in HTTP status interface. Exposes server information and database statistics.
risks: Exposes database names, collection names, server configuration
recommendations: Disable MongoDB HTTP interface (--nohttpinterface)
NEVER expose to internet

49152.description: Windows dynamic/private RPC port range (49152-65535). May expose Windows services.
recommendations: Block ephemeral port range at perimeter firewall
These ports should only be accessible internally
